distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the coefficients.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This assumes both sines contribute a factor of 1, ignoring the coefficients.
This has the wrong sign.
This subtracts the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Using the standard limit \( \frac{1-\cos(kx)}{x^{2}} \to \frac{k^{2}}{2} \), here \( k = [[a]] \), so the limit is \( \frac{[[a]]^{2}}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2.
This uses \( a \) instead of \( a^{2} \) in the numerator.
This assumes the numerator and denominator both vanish to give 0.
This drops the square and the factor of 2 entirely.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Substitute \( u = \frac{[[a]]}{x} \to 0 \): \( x\sin\!\left(\frac{[[a]]}{x}\right) = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{\sin u}{u} \to [[a]]\cdot 1 = [[answer]] \), using the special limit \( \frac{\sin u}{u} \to 1 \) as \( u \to 0 \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the sine term forces the whole product to 0.
This assumes the limit is always 1, ignoring the coefficient \( a \).
This squares the coefficient instead of returning it directly.
This inverts the coefficient instead of returning it directly.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: Combine the two standard limits: \( 1 - \cos([[a]]x) \sim \dfrac{([[a]]x)^{2}}{2} \) and \( \sin([[b]]x) \sim [[b]]x \), so \( \dfrac{1-\cos([[a]]x)}{x\sin([[b]]x)} \to \dfrac{[[a]]^{2}x^{2}/2}{[[b]]x^{2}} = \dfrac{[[a]]^{2}}{2\cdot[[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the square on \( a \) and the factor of 2, treating both special limits as simple ratios.
This forgets the factor of 2 from the cosine limit.
This has \( a \) and \( b \) transposed.
This treats \( a \) linearly and forgets to square it.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

solution: From the Taylor series \( \sin(kx) = kx - \dfrac{(kx)^{3}}{6} + \cdots \), the leading remainder term gives \( \dfrac{\sin([[a]]x) - [[a]]x}{x^{3}} \to -\dfrac{[[a]]^{3}}{6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the wrong sign on the correct value.
This uses \( a \) instead of \( a^{3} \) in the numerator.
This wrongly assumes the numerator and denominator cancel exactly to give 0.
This drops the factor of 6 entirely.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
